title: Plantain fresh poultice
subtitle: A first-aid poultice for insect stings and minor grazes
excerpt: Fresh broadleaf plantain leaves mashed between the palms and pressed onto a minor insect sting, graze, or skin irritation. The oldest and most immediate herbal first-aid preparation in the British countryside.
sourceNotes: Maud Grieve (the early 20th-century botanical writer), A Modern Herbal, plantain entry: the poultice tradition for insect bites, minor wounds, and stings. Nicholas Culpeper (the 17th-century herbalist), The English Physician, plantain entry, the external applications. King's American Dispensatory (Felter & Lloyd, 1898), Plantago major, the wound-supportive and inflammation-calming tradition.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Fresh plantain poultice is always prepared on the spot. Gather the leaves immediately before use.

glossaryTerms.definition: A mashed fresh herb applied directly to the skin. The oldest herbal preparation form: no equipment, no heat, no waiting. The herb's active compounds enter the skin and tissue directly.

content.text: is the simplest first-aid remedy in the British countryside. Two or three broadleaf plantain leaves, gathered from a clean spot, mashed between the palms or bruised with a stone, and pressed onto an insect sting, nettle sting, minor graze, or skin irritation. Native American peoples called plantain 'white man's foot' because it followed European settlement everywhere; it is now present on nearly every grassed footpath, garden edge, and verge in Britain. You can usually find it within 50 metres. The active compounds (allantoin, aucubin, soothing gel) enter the tissue directly; the cooling, drawing action on a wasp sting or nettle rash is noticeable within a few minutes.

attrs.title: Important safety notes
attrs.body:
Not medical advice. Consult a qualified herbalist or doctor for ongoing or serious symptoms.

Plantain has no significant safety concerns for external use. It is among the safest herbs in the western tradition.

Foraging caution. Gather from a site free from dog fouling, herbicides (avoid roadside verges near recently sprayed areas), and air pollution. Rinse the leaves first if possible.

Anaphylaxis. An insect sting in a reader with a known severe sting allergy is a medical emergency; call 999. A plantain poultice is for mild stings in non-allergic readers only.

Infected skin. Do not apply any poultice to infected, hot, swollen, or pus-bearing skin; this needs medical attention.

items.prepNote: fresh broadleaf plantain (Plantago major) or ribwort plantain (P. lanceolata); gather from a clean, unsprayed spot

content.text: Rinse the leaves briefly if possible; a splash from a water bottle or a quick rub with a damp cloth is enough in the field. Mash the leaves between your palms, rubbing them firmly together until the leaf turns dark and slightly slimy. Alternatively, place the leaves on a stone or hard surface and bruise them with another stone or a heel. The leaves should smell green and slightly herbal and feel noticeably slippery. Press the mashed leaf mass directly onto the affected area and hold in place with light pressure or a folded cloth. Leave for 15-30 minutes or until the initial soreness eases.

Apply immediately after the sting or graze and leave for 15-30 minutes. Reapply with fresh leaves if needed. There is no upper limit on applications; the preparation is safe for repeated use on unbroken skin. Suitable for adults and children of all ages for external use.
When not to use this remedy
Suspected anaphylaxis: call 999 immediately. Deep wounds, animal or human bites, infected skin, or significant burns all need medical attention. This preparation is for minor skin events only.
